utterances: …obsessed with.
But anyway, they would feature music, and they would have a little thing at the end saying tonight's music was by, and they would show the artist.
But this was pre-iTunes.
So now what happens now is you see a show on a - see a song - hear a song on a show that you like.
It's well placed.
You can hear the lyrics.
You type in the lyrics, you find the artist, you find the song, you buy the song.
It's this domino effect that happens so quickly, and…
You can do the same thing with McDreamy's jacket, too.
If you like that, you can also find out what it was and buy that, too.
Exactly.
Yeah.
Thanks.
Thanks for the call, Eric.
Is there other stuff that we should know the attribution of?
But I think that's why television is having such a huge impact with music, because they - this isn't anything - it's not like music hasn't been used in television shows before.

I think that in the past 10 years that there has been more of that, but it's just the partnership with the digital side and the immediate gratification that's just zooming these numbers off the chart, you know.
Josh, good luck.
Thank you very much, Neal.
Good luck.
OK, bye-bye.
Let's see if we can get another caller in - if I can find the right button.
There we go, Rock.
And Rock is with us from Payson, Arizona.
Howdy.
I happen to be an artist, but I am a visual artist instead of an audio artist.

And I was wanting to comment on the fact that these new technologies are doing the same thing for us as they are for your recording stars in that we are no longer victims of publishers and galleries who - especially the publishers, who used to take 14 percent of wholesale if we were lucky to get a contract with the major ones.
And nowadays with the new technology and what are called (unintelligible), we get 100 percent of retail if we are smart and do our marketing right.
Hm.
Oh, that's great.
So basically, what we've done is, you know, we've become advocees(ph) - advocates, I should say, of the new technology.
And when people me what my Web site is anymore, I say, well, it's RockNukem.com(ph).
But if you just put my name in and hit your Google, I should show up on 500 sites.
Wow.
Rock, good luck to you, and nicely worked in that plug, there.
That was very sweet.
(Soundbite of laughter)
Thank you so much, Neal.
Bye-bye.
I wonder, when you were writing that song, the one we played earlier, Ingrid, were you - at this point, you were - that was a, you know, that was an assignment.
You were doing that specifically for ""Grey's Anatomy,"" weren't you?
Yeah, well, my manager and I, we were trying to see, you know, what the deal with the soundtrack was and getting a song on the soundtrack.
But because I already had two songs on this season, thankfully, up until that point, so we were like, okay.
We had two chances.

But the only thing is is there were hundreds of other artists that had been played and they were all - I mean, anybody that watches that show knows that they have an excellent taste in music, so it was hard, you know, competition that you're up against.
So we inquired, and we found that they were looking for original music also to put on the soundtrack.
So I'm thinking, okay, you know, maybe I should just write a song and see what happens.
Maybe they'll place it, and then maybe it'll get on the soundtrack, who knows.
And you did.
So, basically, I did, which is kind of crazy, because I've never sat down to write a song specifically for anything except for just writing a song, you know.
And while I definitely had in my mind I have to slant this towards a more general, not a very Ingrid specific - you know, I wanted to make it a little more general.
Mm-hmm.

And it ended up coming out to be a really organic expression of what I've been going through recently, so I'm really happy that I actually sat down and tried to do something that I'm not used to doing.
It was really, really great.
Ingrid Michaelson, good luck to you.
Thanks for being with us.
Thank you very much.
Ingrid Michaelson is a musician based in New York City.
Show joined us from NPR's bureau in New York.
This is TALK OF THE NATION from NPR News.
I'm Madeleine Brand, and this is DAY TO DAY.
The women's studies department at the University of Washington has a new chairman--yes, man.
Professor David G.
Allen will assume the post in September.
He'll be the first male chair of a women's studies program at a major American university.
And welcome to DAY TO DAY, Professor Allen.
Professor DAVID G.
ALLEN (Incoming Chair, Women's Studies Department, University of Washington in Seattle): Well, thank you.
I'm pleased to be here.
So how did you, a man, end up chairing this department?
I've worked in the women's studies department probably for 15 years here, and before that I was associated with women's studies at the University of Wisconsin in Madison.
So I'm a known quantity to them.
In addition to that, I had been a department chair in my home department in nursing.
And the additional piece of it that's probably more troubling is that there was really a fairly small pool of people who want the job.
Some people were troubled by it, and I think totally understandably.
Some people still are.
But most people, the vast majority, said, `Yes, please stay in the pool.
We at least want you as a candidate.
And then the search committee interviewed me and other people and made a recommendation to the dean, who interviewed me and other people, and I was selected.
And those who were troubled with it were basically saying, `Well, it's just wrong to have a man chair a women's studies department--it's just an example of the gender bias that's in the academy'?
I think so, yeah.
I think they have a number of points.
Given the fact that there are so few women in administrative positions, this seems like an obvious position to offer to a woman.
And the second one is, they didn't think that a male coming through kind of privilege that men have in this culture would really understand the kinds of struggles and content of women's studies.

The third one is just the symbolic one, which is this is a department that has been organized around feminism and the study of the various ways in which cultures disadvantage women, and it seemed wrong to some people to have a man leading that department.
Well, I wonder, if the university searched the entire country, that there wasn't one qualified female professor to take in?
Is even more going to change before the election in November?
This intensely local labor conflict really isn't that local at all, says Ron Bennett.
He's the president of School Services of California, an educational consulting group based in Sacramento.
Bennett says San Francisco's problems are emblematic of the educational funding crisis throughout California.

The fact is, the state of California simply isn't funding public education at the same level that other states in the nation are funding public education, but we're faced with even greater costs than most other states have, and as a result, there isn't enough on the table to serve everybody that appears for dinner, so we do get some pretty abrupt confrontations.
For now, San Francisco Mayor Gavin Newsom is hoping to pre-empt a strike with informal talks with both the union and the school district.
Richard Gonzales, NPR News, San Francisco.
This is NPR News.
From NPR News, this is ALL THINGS CONSIDERED.
I'm Andrea Seabrook.
And I'm Michele Norris.
For decades now, the U.S.
has invested billions of dollars in high-tech warfare, things like precision-guided weapon systems and unmanned aerial vehicles.
It turns out that those weapons have their limits in Iraq, where the enemy can easily blend in with civilians and the battlefield is often a crowded urban street.

A recent article in the National Journal magazine looks at the military's challenge in dealing with so-called intimate killing, a term coined by retired Army war college commandant Major General Robert Scales - a frequent guest on this program.
Sydney Freedberg Jr. wrote the article and he says the military is now spending more effort training soldiers to engage in intimate killing and training them in to deal with the aftermath.
Mr.
SYDNEY FREEDBERG JR. (Staff Correspondent, National Journal): A lot of the combat is a few feet away.

I've talked to soldiers about - well, the young Special Forces captain included in the article, Chris Morris(ph), who's got two Bronze Stars for valor, was charging over a low hill, a small rise, and he was, all of a sudden, within about two or three feet from four Iraqis - Iraqi troops.
He had been trained so intensively that he was moving even as he ran forward in this very chaotic, stressful situation with his rifle to his shoulder.
They hadn't been trained to the same degree so they were carrying their rifles at the hip.
That gave him about a three or four second advantage.
And he put his rifle to each one in turn at about two feet of distance and shot them all.
Now, this guy is an exceptional soldier.
Everybody I've talked to agrees with that.
But that speaks to the intimacy of the violence, and also how the military's obsessive emphasis on training, you know, every bit of detail.
How is the military, since we're talking about a case where the battlefield is often a city street, a densely populated urban environment.
How is the military training the troops to handle this kind of combat?
Mr.

FREEDBURG JR.: It's very difficult, because the traditional approach that we learned in World War II for dealing with the city was you pound the hell out of it with artillery and you don't move in the streets because in the streets you're exposed.
You'll get shot.
You move from building to building by blowing through the wall, and you go in through every door firing.
And if there are any civilians around, well, that's really too bad.
